speaker: interviewer
text: The children, I want you to talk about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: At the very-- at number four. OK, so you were making your way back to Budapest. And you found your sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I found my sister and my father. I can't count my blessing that three of us from the same family. Sometime, I think that this helped me keep my sanity, that three of us from one family, which is so many times, it's just unheard of. And then besides this, from around 25 also people around the Seder table, only two cousins of mine lived and live. One of them in New York, and one is in Argentina, which we keep in contact with. Actually, my cousin in New York, I met after 53 years, three years ago. We were in contact. But we never exchanged pictures. And I wouldn't have recognized him for a million dollars. You know, I would've walked by him. It was like total strangers. But it was 53 years because 1939 I saw him last. And then a few years back, we met up again. He came to Portland. And so we tried together our lives together. My father started working. And I went back to learn my trade. My sister went to school to finish his education. And I finished my trade school. And I started working as a machinist. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] But things, they were hard. It's no question. It was freer. But it wasn't free, because in 1948, the Communists took over Hungary. And even though it was not openly anti-semitism, but it I was hidden anti-semitism still around us. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] It was hard. It was. My father never-- he actually didn't give up finding my, my mother and my little sister for years. He went to all over, wrote letters to Red Crosses all over in the world, tried to find him, find them. But in myself, I knew their fate. I, I knew that. And it was such that I didn't even wanted to talk about it. And I really didn't talk about it for 35 years, until my children were old enough. About 15 years ago, I told my children what, what we went through. And at one occasion, my son-- I'm rushing ahead of myself. But as I say, in Budapest, it was-- the change was good. But it's not good enough. And in 1956, when the Hungarian Revolution broke out, by then I got married. That was six months before the revolution broke out. Met my wife, and we got married. And we decided when the revolution was beaten down by the Communists again that we leave the country. And so my father, my stepmother-- my father remarried by then, by 1956. Christmas Eve of 1956, we crossed over to Austria in knee-deep snow, which is another saga. But because of my stepbrother's son was here in Portland, we were allowed to come to Portland as refugees. This is how we settled in Portland. I always called the trip to the United States my honeymoon, even though it was six months later. It was glorious. And i count my blessings ever since every day. And so I started working as a machinist here in Portland, worked 18 years in one place, and then 30 years at Tektronix here in the computer company, in research and development, building prototypes. And my son born in 1960. And my daughter born in 1960 and my son in '64.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are your children's names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Excuse me?

speaker: interviewer
text: What are your children's names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My daughter, Suzanne, and my son, Robert.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your wife? What is your wife's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eva. Eva Erika. And so basically, it was then when I was able to sleep through a night. Because before that, I had so many sleepless night. I woke up dreaming that I'm back again. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Woke up horrifying, like screaming. And when my children born, my thought of direction changed. And we were a family. And we got busy and with the family. And, and so slowly, I were able to sleep through the nights. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Then as I mentioned, my son, at one time when he was 15 years old, came home from high school, that they learning, talking about the Holocaust, if I would go into the school and talk about it. I was so hesitant about it because I never talked about it then till then. That was in [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] about '79. He was 15. And I went in. And one occasion, I don't know, with my broken English at that time-- I talking too much Hungarian with my wife-- and so I told my experiences. And then I again shut up for many years. And just a year-and-a-half ago, I was called up by the Oregon Holocaust Resource Center, if I would want-- would like to or would do some talk to school children or groups of people in conjunction with the Anne Frank exhibit, which were traveling all over in Oregon. And I agreed to it. And from since then, I going out to groups, I mean, to schools and churches and colleges and different places where they invite me. And they getting my name from the Holocaust Resource Center to talk about my Holocaust experiences.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you feel about talking about them now?

speaker: interviewee
text: How do I feel about--

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you feel about talking? How do you feel about talking to these students?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first, as I said, was hesitant. I didn't know-- I never been a speaker. And I am still not. I'm just telling my experiences, really. I was very hesitant. And-- but as more I talk about it, and I getting these letters from schoolchildren, I get more relieved and I can talk about it more calmly. And I realize that it has to be told. . And people appreciate it. And the, the feedback I get, it helps me overcome. It reinstates my trust in people, in a way, because I was so mistrusting. And actually, my children probably can tell the same thing that I all these years, I told them, just lay low. You don't have to tell that you are Jewish. I still, this feeling was with me for many, many years. Not anymore. I'm not ashamed. As a matter of fact, I'm not as religious as I used to be. But I'm more of a Jew I used to be.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know that you have some letters. And we have some photographs that you want to show.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I have some photographs about my family way back when, which was saved by my sister. She were able to get at pass out of this encampment and from the factory, and went back to the ghetto. And in about a week later that we were taken. And I have a letter from her, which she wrote to my father, that I went back to the ghetto. And it's a dismal place. I didn't find anything, just a few pictures and a few papers I were able to pick up, because all the rest of the stuff what we had were taken away. And she wrote this letter to my father in that war camp where he was. They were in contact to the last minute.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, first, let me take this moment when it's just you and me to thank you very much for sharing your story and sharing so much of your life. I can't tell you how much I appreciate it. We will show the photographs. I think-- do you want me to give him some letters before we-- do you want to stop down for a minute? Mr. Aigner, I know that you have some letters in your hand. Do you want to explain to us what these letters are?

speaker: interviewee
text: All these letters here from schools and institutions where I went to talk to people about the Holocaust in conjunction with the Frank-- Anne Frank exhibit. And so these schools are invited speakers from the Holocaust Resource Center. And I'm one of them who lectures about the Holocaust and telling my experiences in the camp. And I want to read you just a few. This is a sixth grader, Angie Madares. 'Dear Mr. And Mrs. Aigner, I appreciate you coming and sharing your experience. It made me think about what you had to go through. Thank you for coming and sharing the dramatic story with the school. Just wanted to let you know how much your braveness means to me. Glad you could come to our school and sharing it with us. Thank you very much for coming. Sincerely, Angie.' 'Dear Leslie and Eva, Thank you very much for coming and talking to our class about your life. I'm a big fan of Holocaust books and stories. Someday, I hope to go to the Holocaust Museum. It's on the East Coast. I find your story very interesting.'

speaker: interviewer
text: Are there any others you'd like to read? I know you have, you have a lot.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have hundreds of them, probably 400 here. This is probably a teacher, Cynthia [INAUDIBLE], a teacher of one of the schools. 'Dear Leslie, I want to thank you.' I want to say that when we go out to talk-- I say we. My wife and I both go because she went through the Holocaust in the Budapest ghetto. And she have her experiences to tell. So she speaks about 10 minutes. And I speak about 45 minutes. Then we have a question and answer period. And it's very interesting to see these young people asking very interesting questions.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, you know what maybe we could do before we bring Robert and Sue back? Maybe your wife-- how would you like your wife to-- would you like your wife to be with you? Or would she like to talk by herself about the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wouldn't mind. She can have their own experience. And it's not-- that's pretty dramatic, too, the way she survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, well, we'll do her a little bit later. But we're going to do-- we're going to film your photographs now.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK? And, and then we'll bring your-- and then we'll bring out your wife. And then we'll bring your children out.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you very much, Mr. Aigner.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome. Thank you for.

speaker: interviewer
text: Change in plans here. Since Mr Aigner's wife is also a survivor, but was quite young, we're going to bring her out and talk to her for a few minutes as part of his tape. And then the children will come in, Robert and Sue, and we'll all talk together. And then we will do the photographs. Tape, Leslie Aigner's tape. And we're going to spend a few minutes with his wife, Eva Aigner. Before we begin, I just want to get a little information from you. Can you please tell me your full name, including your maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, my full name is Eva Erika Aigner. And my maiden name was Eva Erika Spiegel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell Spiegel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, S-P-I-E-G-E-L.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you tell me what city and-- when you were born, first of all, the full year, the date, the month?

speaker: interviewee
text: I born in 1937, June 17th, in Czechoslovakia, a town named Kosice.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Kosice, was it eventually annexed? And did it become part of Hungary?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was sort of a part of the country which was thrown back and forth all the time. At times, it was Czechoslovakia. And sometimes, it was Hungarian. When I was born, it was Czechoslovakian.

speaker: interviewer
text: And today, what is it a part-- is it Slovakia or--

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not sure I know if it's Czech or Slovakia. One or the other.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, according to your husband, who made it clear that you don't-- that you speak briefly, I don't want you feel as though you have to be as brief as your husband seems to make it.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: But he said that you speak at schools.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: I wondered if you could tell me a little bit about your parents and just your experiences. Because you were so young, and I realize that your memory bank is probably fairly limited as a result.

speaker: interviewee
text: But as I mentioned, I born in 1937. And my father, his name was Maurice. He was a hat maker. He was a small businessman. He had been self-employed. And my mother was a homemaker. I had a sister who was eight years older. Her name was [? Eboya. ?] And they lived a very middle class life. And seems like my grandparents also lived within a couple of blocks. And my father's brothers and the family very close by. It was a very close knit and a very religious family. In 1939, my father's business license was taken away because he was a Jew. And he really tried to get a job in Kosice to support his family. But nobody would hire him. Being a small town, everybody knew he was Jewish. And nobody would give him a job. Well, of course, he was running out of funds. And he had two little children. And he was corresponding with one of his brothers who lived in Budapest, Hungary, and told him what happened to us, that his license was taken away, and he didn't have a means anymore to support his family.

speaker: interviewee
text: So his brother said, you know, Budapest is a bigger city. Your chances of getting a job would be much easier. So he loaded up his little furniture and the family. And he moved us up to Budapest. And that was in 1939. Well, about that time in Hungary also, licenses were not given any more to Jews. So he ended up finding a job. And he worked for a hat maker. But this man wouldn't hire him in such a way that he would work in his shop. He was bringing home the material and working it up at home because this man didn't want to have a Jew working for him also. And he made a living like that until 1944.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I went to first grade, that was the first time I experienced being different than anybody else. And I felt discriminated. In Hungary, when the school day started, every day when you got up, they said the national prayer, the whole class. And one day to the next, they said, Jewish children, please stand up. And there was about seven or eight of us in the class. And we were ordered out of the class. We were told that the Jews no longer can say the prayers with the rest of the class. And even though I was very little, about seven years old, I remembered how humiliated we were, and how awful it felt that we could no longer pray with the rest of our friends. And I remember going home and crying and, and telling my mother. And by that time, it was so much anti-Jewish movement going on, she didn't want any trouble. And she says, well, you just have to do as the teacher says. In the meanwhile, my father was taken to a work camp, where, unfortunately, he never returned from. He was killed. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what the camp was that we was sent to?

speaker: interviewee
text: The last place he was, it was called the [? Veszprem. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Which is-- do you know where [? Veszprem ?] was?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a part of Hungary. And this is the last time we heard that he was there. And they took a lot of people into mass graves. So we don't exactly know. And then we were living still in the apartment where we lived, my mother, my sister, and I. And one day they started to gather the old people and the, the women, and the children into buildings which was marked with a yellow star. We were wearing the yellow star. And the buildings were also marked with the yellow star. But this wasn't the ghetto yet. It was just the gathering people for the Jews. And we lived in this building, I don't know how long. And one day, the Hungarian Aerocross man with the swastika on the arm came along. And they picked out the still able-bodied women from, from this building, because all the men were taken already. And only the children and the very old people were left. And they picked my mother out also. She was 38 years old. And my sister, who wasn't quite 15, and I, I was 7 1/2, was left behind alone. And of course, it was a terrible parting because we pretty much knew already that our father was gone. And to think that two children, the two of us, were left behind. And my mother cried and screamed. And I never forget this moment when we were parted. We, we felt so horribly alone and isolated. Well, my mother was taken. We didn't know where. But as it turned out to be, they took her to the brick factory where they put the people on the trains to take them to Auschwitz. And my mother was put on the train toward Auschwitz. And the first time when they opened the train door, my mother jumped out and escaped. A German soldier were marked a SS, captured my mother on a gunpoint. And my mother spoke several languages. She spoke Yiddish. She spoke a little German, Czechoslovakian, Hungarian. And she got on her knees. And she begged this man, this soldier. She says, please let me go. He says, I have two little children at home. If I don't get back, they never survive this war. And somehow, this man got touched. And he pulled out the picture from his wallet. And he said, look, I have three children at home and a wife. I don't like to fight this war. He says, I give you a chance. And he turned around and let her go. Well, after several days of walking, and she tore off her yellow star because didn't want anybody to know she was Jewish. And on the train, she got back to Budapest. She went back to the apartment where we were, where she was taken from. And there was nobody left there. She find out from some neighbors that just on that morning, they emptied the building. And they took all the children and the old people down by the Danube. She scrambled down to the direction where she was told that we could be. And they were shooting all the children and the old people into the Danube because they didn't want to bother with them anymore. They just wanted to get rid of as many Jews as possible. And to this day, I still think it's a miracle, because my sister and I were standing in the line. And the only thing I remember, it was terribly cold. And I had a blanket on my shoulder. And we heard the shooting. But we really didn't know that this was the sound because they were shooting the children into the river and the people. We were so accustomed to all the shooting and all the bombing that we didn't know what was happening. And my mother somehow heard my sister's crying voice from the line and recognized it. And I still think, you know, this is a miracle of miracles. There is no other explanation why this happened. And she pulled off her wedding ring and gave it to the guard. He says, please let these two children out of the line. And he did. And he says, I can't let Jews wander around in the city. He says, the only way I can let you out of the line if I take you to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he escorted us on gunpoint to the ghetto, my mother, my sister, and I. And that's where we survived the war until we were liberated by the Russians in Hungary. And by the time we were liberated, we were in terrible shape. We had dysentery. And conditions were really bad.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we did survive, the three of us, and went back to our apartment were we originally lived. We couldn't find nothing. Everything was totally taken and confiscated. The first night, we didn't even have a blanket to cover with. We slept on the floor. But we were so thrilled that we were alive and we made it. And this is what happened to me through the war. And this is how I survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your recollections are rather limited because you were eight when the war ended. Correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: I do want to ask you, though, a couple questions.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you were put into the ghetto, where did you live in Budapest? How did-- were you Buda, Pest, where were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Pest side. In Pest side. And it's-- actually, each time we've been back, I went back because, you know, in Hungary, people still don't move from the apartments. And, and I have one cousin who also survived. And she still lives in the apartment what we used to have.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I still go back in the area. And it brings up some pretty bad memories. If we wouldn't have some family left in Hungary, I probably wouldn't even feel like going back. I know you were very young. But do you have any recollections? Do you remember Raoul Wallenburg being there when you were little and him saving people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you know, I, I have recollection of a couple of things when I was-- at that time. I remember that people were being told that if you would change your religion, if you go through Catechism, that might give you a chance to, you know, survive in such a way that you wouldn't be taken to the ghetto or to the camps. Well, my mother came from a very orthodox, very, very, religious family. And she absolutely didn't want hear of it. She said that she felt that if she did that, her life would be over anyhow. So I know that. And we also heard that you could buy papers to get into the Swiss houses. But she didn't have the money. We didn't have the funds to do that because after my-- ever since my father lived in Budapest, from '39 to '44, he just barely made ends meet. So we heard about it. But [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] nothing really happened as far as saving us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then you went into the actual ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Once, once you were saved from the Danube, you then went into--

speaker: interviewee
text: The ghetto. And we were there about three months.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then the war was over?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then the war was over. And the one thing I distinctly remember-- it's funny as a child what stays in your mind. We had-- it, It was maybe 20, 30 blocks from the area where we lived, but we had to walk from the ghetto. But in my mother's condition, she was trying to partially carry me and then carry my sister. And we were all trying to help each other, because these blocks seemed like we would never get there. And was we were walking, there was a dead horse laying on the street, which evidently in the last moments of fighting, as the Russians were taking over Budapest, was shot. Well, the people-- as we were walking out from the ghetto, people were so starved. In the last few weeks of the fighting, it like they even forget about the ghetto. We hardly got our daily food over there. Then before you know it, in front of my eye, that horse became a skeleton, because people carved off the meat in minutes. And they carried it home so they at least would have some food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now we're coming to the end of the tape. Do you have any other things that you would like to continue with, because we can start-- we will start you on the next tape if there are some-- if there are few more things you want to talk about.

speaker: interviewee
text: What I would like to add that--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we'll do it on the next tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
